blocks.text: Heather Smith, Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}
Re: Habitat Restoration Monitoring, Post-Construction Compliance Services
Dear Ms. Smith:

This letter sets out the terms on which Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC will carry out habitat restoration monitoring for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}. It follows our walk of the restoration area and the as-built drawings, planting plan, and permit conditions your office sent afterward. Signed and returned, it is the agreement between us for this work, and it stands in place of anything either of us said before it. Correspondence and invoices will go to your attention at Carter and Sons unless you tell us otherwise.
I have kept it short. Where it hedges, it hedges because the work does, and I would rather say so now than in a report.
Scope of Services

The permit conditions require that the restoration area be monitored and that the results be reported to the agency in a form it can check. Our services cover one monitoring event and the report that documents it. Specifically:

items: Reconnaissance of the restoration area, with the planted limits verified against the approved plan and permanent photo stations established at fixed bearings so that this year's photographs can be compared with next year's.

Vegetation monitoring in fixed plots: species composition, areal cover by stratum, and a count of surviving installed stock, recorded plot by plot and tallied against the performance standards named in the permit.
Hydrologic monitoring: shallow groundwater wells and the staff gauge read on a fixed interval, with inundation observed at the plot locations and referenced to the predicted tide.
Sediment sampling at the outfall and at the reference location, collected under chain of custody and delivered to the analytical laboratory with holding times documented on the form.
Mapping of invasive species within the restoration area and a fifty-foot buffer around it, by patch, with areal extent estimated in the field.
One monitoring report suitable for submission to the permitting agency, with the field data tabulated, the photographs keyed to the stations, and each performance standard addressed on its own terms.

blocks.text: Our services do not include maintenance, replanting, remediation design, negotiation with the agency, appearance at a hearing, or expert testimony. Those are useful things and we do them, but not under this letter and not for this fee.
Our Approach

Plots are located by coordinate and marked so that the same ground is read each visit; a monitoring record whose plots wander is a record of the plots, not of the marsh. Cover is estimated visually by two observers working the same plot and reconciled on the spot rather than averaged afterward. Wells are read with a calibrated meter and the calibration is logged the morning of the reading. Samples are labelled at the point of collection, not at the truck.

The estuary sets the schedule. A field day that falls on the wrong tide or the wrong weather produces data we would have to caveat, and a caveat in a monitoring report is a question the reviewer asks you three years later. We will move a field day sooner than collect something we would not defend. You should expect that to happen at least once, and it is not a delay in any sense that matters to the permit.

We report what was measured, where, and on what date. Where the data support a conclusion we will state it. Where they support a range, we will give the range. We will not tell you the restoration is succeeding because the planting looks well; that is an impression, and the agency is not buying impressions from us.
Project Team

Three people from this office will do the work. William Schwartz runs the field program: the boat, the wells, the gauge, the meters, and the calibration of anything that reads a number. He sets the field days and he moves them, and I back him when he does. James Henderson runs the sampling program and the chain of custody, and he writes the report; he is hard on data that arrive too clean, which is the temperament this work wants. I review every deliverable before it leaves the office and I ask where a total came from before I ask what it means. The transmittal carries my name and the responsibility with it.
Fee and Commencement

Our fee for the services described above is {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope as written and inclusive of laboratory analysis, boat time, mileage, and reproduction. Work commences on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}} and runs approximately three months from commencement through delivery of the monitoring report.
Fees and Payment

We invoice monthly for the portion of the work completed. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. Work outside the scope above will not be started until you have authorized it in writing and we have quoted it in writing; a request in the field is not an authorization, and William Schwartz has instructions to say so politely and keep working on what is in this letter.
Term and Termination

This agreement runs from commencement through delivery of the monitoring report and our written response to one round of agency comments on it. Either party may terminate on ten days' written notice. On termination you pay for work performed and for commitments already made on your behalf, principally laboratory charges for samples in the queue. Field data, photographs, and any samples in our custody at termination are delivered to you.
Confidentiality

The data we collect and the reports we prepare for you are yours, and we hold them in confidence except where a regulatory agency compels disclosure or a court orders it. We ask one thing in return: our reports travel to third parties whole or not at all. A cover figure quoted out of the report loses the qualifications that make it true.
Limitation of Liability

Monitoring is a sample of a place at a time. Conditions between the plots and between the visits are inferred from what the plots and the visits show; they are not measured, and no field program of a size anyone would pay for measures them. Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement is limited to the amount of our fee. We do not warrant that the restoration will meet its performance standards. We report whether the measurements say it did, which is the only thing we are in a position to warrant and the only thing the agency will credit.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the law of the state in which the restoration area lies, without regard to its conflict-of-laws provisions. Before either of us files anything, we will sit down with a mediator we both agree on. In this business most disputes are disputes about what was meant, and those are cheaper to settle in a room.

If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign below and return one copy to my attention; we will countersign and scan it to the project file. If anything in it reads wrong to you, call me and we will fix it before you sign rather than argue about it later. We are glad to be doing this work.
Very truly yours,
Memorandum
To: Project team, Habitat Restoration Monitoring
From: William Schwartz, Field Technician
Subject: Kickoff, monitoring program for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}

Work commenced {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}. This memo is the plan we agreed to in the office so that nobody has to reconstruct it from memory in April. The objective is one monitoring event and one report the agency can check line by line: vegetation, hydrology, sediment, invasives, photographs. Everything below serves that. Anything not below is out of scope and goes to Angela before we touch it.
Workstreams

items: Plot and station layout. I set the vegetation plots and the photo stations off the as-built and the planting plan, mark them, and record coordinates. Stations get a bearing written on the tag. Same ground, same bearing, every visit, or the photographs are worth nothing next year.

Hydrology. Wells and the staff gauge on a fixed interval. Meter calibrated the morning of each reading and the calibration written in the book before we leave the office, not after. Readings referenced to the predicted tide for that day.

Vegetation. Composition, cover by stratum, survival counts of installed stock, plot by plot. Two observers on each plot, reconciled in the plot. James Henderson runs the call on species we disagree about.

Sediment. Outfall and reference location. James Henderson owns the chain of custody end to end, including labelling at the point of collection and holding times on the form. Nothing gets labelled at the truck. If a hold is blown we say so in the report and we do not use the number.
Invasives. Patch mapping in the restoration area and the fifty-foot buffer. Extent estimated in the field, sketched on the aerial, tabulated after.
Report. James Henderson drafts. Angela Flynn reviews before anything goes out. Method first, then what we measured, then each performance standard on its own terms.

blocks.text: Field days

I schedule them off the tide and the forecast, not off the calendar. If the water or the weather is wrong, the day moves. That is in the engagement letter and the client has been told to expect it, so nobody needs to negotiate it with me on the morning. What I will not do is collect a number I would have to caveat in the report.

Boat, meters, and the well tape are mine. They get checked the day before, not the morning of. Anyone who needs equipment on a field day tells me the week before. The tide predictions for the whole monitoring window are already pulled and in the folder, so the candidate days are knowable in advance and there is no reason for anyone to be surprised by one.
Next steps

items: I lay out plots and photo stations on the first workable tide and circulate the coordinate list to the file.
James Henderson confirms the laboratory's turnaround and holding times before the first sampling day and puts the chain-of-custody forms in the truck box.
I pull the tide predictions for the monitoring window and post the candidate field days on the board. Candidate, not committed.
Angela Flynn reviews the plot layout against the permit conditions before we read the first plot.
Invoicing and any question about the terms go to Heather Smith at Carter and Sons. Scope questions come to Angela Flynn first. Neither of those is a field decision and neither is mine.

blocks.text: Field books stay in the truck box and get copied to the project folder the day they are used. If it is not written down it did not happen, and if it happened three weeks ago and is not written down, it did not happen either.
Meeting Minutes: Habitat Restoration Monitoring

Working session, office and telephone. Minutes taken by James Henderson, Environmental Scientist. Subject: monitoring program for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}. These minutes are the record; corrections to me in writing within five business days.
Attendees

items: James Henderson, Environmental Scientist, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC (minutes)
William Schwartz, Field Technician, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC
Heather Smith, Chief Financial Officer, Carter and Sons (by telephone)

blocks.text: Discussion

Plot and photo station layout is complete and the coordinate list is in the project folder. William Schwartz reported two vegetation plots relocated from their planned positions: the planned positions fell on a spoil ridge that is not part of the restoration grading and would have reported cover the planting never established. The relocation is documented in the field book with the reason, and it will be stated in the report rather than left for a reviewer to discover by comparing coordinates against the plan.

First round of well and gauge readings is in hand. Calibration records accompany each reading. Two readings from one well came back tighter than the surrounding wells across a tide that should have moved them. I am treating that as a suspect instrument rather than a suspect marsh until it is checked. William Schwartz will re-read the well with a second tape and, if the readings do not reconcile, the well is pulled from the data set for that interval and the report says so. I would rather flag it and explain it than let it into a table unremarked.

Sediment sampling: the first round is collected and delivered, holding times documented on the form, custody unbroken. Laboratory turnaround was confirmed before collection. No results are in hand yet and none will be discussed with the client until they are, including in summary and including verbally.

Invasive mapping is partially complete. Buffer patches on the landward side are larger than the planting plan anticipated. That is an observation about extent, not yet a finding about the restoration; whether it bears on the performance standards is a question the second round of vegetation data will answer, and it will be addressed then, in the report, and not before.

Heather Smith asked whether an interim letter could be sent to the agency ahead of the report. Position taken: no. An interim letter on partial data invites the agency to hold us to a number we have not finished measuring. The monitoring report is the deliverable named in the permit conditions, and it goes when it is complete and reviewed. Heather Smith accepted this and asked that the report note the mapping observation explicitly so that Carter and Sons is not surprised by it. Agreed.
Heather Smith also asked about invoicing against the schedule discussed. Referred to Angela Flynn; not a field or laboratory question and not answered in this meeting.
Action Items

rows: Re-read the suspect well with a second tape; reconcile or pull the interval from the data set and document the reason in the field book.
Circulate the revised plot coordinate list with the relocation and its justification noted.
Log laboratory results against the chain-of-custody forms on receipt; verify holding times before any result is tabulated.
Complete invasive patch mapping in the landward buffer and tabulate extent by patch.
Draft the report method section against the field books to date, so the methods are written from the record and not from memory.
Before the next round of data
Refer the client's invoicing question to Angela Flynn.

blocks.text: No commercial terms were discussed or agreed in this meeting.
Status Report: Habitat Restoration Monitoring
Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} by William Schwartz, Field Technician. Covers the period since the last report. Reviewed by Angela Flynn before issue.
Status Summary

The monitoring program is on scope and on schedule at roughly the halfway point. Plots and photo stations are established and being read on the interval. Hydrologic readings are accumulating. The first sediment round is at the laboratory. Invasive mapping is most of the way through. Nothing has happened that changes the deliverable or the approach set out in the engagement letter.
One data issue is open and is described under Risks. It is the kind of thing that gets found in the middle of a program and fixed there, which is why we read the wells more than once.

Nothing in this report is a finding. A monitoring program at this stage has measurements and not conclusions, and the conclusions belong in the report the permit asks for, where each performance standard gets addressed against the data that bear on it. What follows is an account of what has been measured, where, and with what, so that you know what you are paying for while it is happening.
Accomplishments This Period

items: Vegetation plots and permanent photo stations laid out, marked, and coordinated. Two plots were moved off their planned positions onto restoration ground; the planned positions fell on a spoil ridge outside the graded area and would have reported cover the planting never put there. The move and the reason are in the field book and will be in the report.
Photographs taken at every station on the recorded bearing. These are the baseline the next several years of monitoring get compared against, which is why the bearings are written on the tags.
Wells and the staff gauge read on the interval, with the meter calibrated the morning of each reading and the calibration logged before leaving the office.
First sediment round collected at the outfall and the reference location, delivered under unbroken chain of custody, holding times documented on the form. James Henderson ran the custody end to end.
Invasive patch mapping substantially complete in the restoration area; the landward buffer is the remainder.
Two field days moved for tide and weather. Both were rescheduled inside the same week and neither cost the program anything. That is the schedule working as intended, not slipping.

blocks.text: Upcoming Work

items: Second round of vegetation readings: composition, cover by stratum, survival counts of installed stock, two observers per plot, reconciled in the plot.
Continued well and gauge readings on the interval, referenced to the predicted tide for the day.
Laboratory results received, logged against the custody forms, and checked for holding times before any of it is tabulated. James Henderson does not tabulate a number he cannot account for.
Invasive mapping completed in the landward buffer and extent tabulated by patch.

Report drafting: method section written from the field books, then the data, then each performance standard addressed on its own terms. James Henderson drafts; Angela Flynn reviews before anything leaves this office.

blocks.text: Budget

The fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}} is unchanged. Nothing in this period required work outside the scope of the engagement letter, so nothing in this period has been charged outside that fee. Laboratory analysis, boat time, and mileage remain inside it. If something arises that falls outside the scope, you will get it in writing from Angela Flynn before it is started, not on an invoice afterward.
Risks

One well is reading tighter than its neighbors across a tide that should have moved it. The likely cause is the instrument, not the marsh. It gets re-read with a second tape on the next field day. If the readings do not reconcile, that well is out of the data set for the affected interval and the report says which interval and why. This is the honest handling of it; a reading we cannot account for does not go into a table on the theory that it is probably fine.

Invasive extent in the landward buffer is larger than the planting plan anticipated. Whether it bears on the performance standards is a question for the second round of vegetation data. It is noted here so that nobody at Carter and Sons reads it for the first time in the report.

Weather and tide remain the schedule risk they were at the start. The monitoring window is what it is, and a bad stretch would compress the field days rather than extend the window. We build the schedule with room for that, and so far the room has been enough.
No laboratory results are reported here, in summary or otherwise, because none are in hand. When they are, they go in the report.
